2019年4月，Facebook正在准备禁止互联网最臭名昭着的误导和仇恨的蔓延，Infowars创始人Alex Jones。然后首席执行官马克扎克伯格亲自干预。
 琼斯获得了臭名昭着的耻辱，声称2012年Sandy Hook小学大屠杀是一个“巨大的恶作剧”，2018年乐园射击的青少年幸存者是“危机演员”。但Facebook发现，他也无情地对抗各种团体的仇恨，包括穆斯林和跨越人。这种行为有资格从公司下的社交网络驱逐和＃39;＆＃34;危险个人和组织，＆＃34;这需要Facebook还删除对他们表示“赞美或支持”的任何内容。
 但是，根据熟悉该决定的人说，Zuckerberg没有考虑讨厌的创始人是一个讨厌的人物，所以他向自己的内部专家推翻了一个巨大的漏洞：Facebook将永久禁止琼斯和他的公司 - 但不会触及从其他Facebook用户的赞美和支持的职位。这意味着琼斯的追随者可以继续在世界上最大的社交网络中分享他的谎言。
 ＆＃34;马克个人不喜欢惩罚，所以他改变了规则，“一位前政策员工告诉Buzzfeed新闻，并指出原始规则已经使用并代表了多个团队之间的未计入工作时间的工作时间和专家。
    “这是我第一次遇到创建一个新的政策，以适应扎克伯格想要的东西。当我们建立一个政策时，它有点低位化，它经历了严格的周期。喜欢，他妈的是什么？“第二届前政策雇员，就像第一个一样，被要求不被命名，所以他们可以谈论内部事项。
 “马克呼吁采取更细微的政策和执法战略，”Facebook发言人Andy Stone据说Alex Jones决定，这也影响了其他极端主义人物的禁令。
 Zuckerberg的“更细微的政策”掀起了级联效应，这两个前雇员说，这延迟了公司努力去除誓言守护者等右翼武装组织，这些组织涉及1月6日在美国国会大学上的第6次起义。也是Facebook愿意改变其规则来安抚美国的右翼并避免政治急诊的案例研究。 
BuzzFeed新闻获得的内部文件以及对14名现任和前任员工的采访显示，在全球公共政策副总裁乔尔·卡普兰（Joel Kaplan）和扎克伯格的异想天开的指导下，公司的政策团队如何发挥巨大的影响力，同时又阻碍了内容审核决策，阻碍产品的发​​展。推出，并代表违反Facebook规定的受欢迎的保守派人士进行干预。
 去年12月，一位前核心数据科学家撰写了一份备忘录，标题为“对内容政策的政治影响”。在BuzzFeed新闻中看到，该备忘录指出，卡普兰的政策小组“定期保护强大的选民”，并列举了几个示例，包括：取消右翼页面上错误信息的处罚，减少旨在提高News Feed中内容质量的尝试以及暂时阻止提案在美国大选之前停止推荐政治团体。
 自从11月的投票以来，至少有六名Facebook员工辞职，辞去了领导层未能就错误信息和仇恨言论留意自己的专家的告辞。即将离任的四名员工明确指出该政策组织是他们工作的障碍，并要求进行重组，以便负责监督游说和政府关系的公共政策小组以及负责制定和执行平台规则的内容政策小组不会两者兼而有之向卡普兰报告。
 Facebook拒绝让Kaplan或其他高管接受采访。公司发言人斯通（Stone）消除了对副总裁影响力的担忧。
 他说：“对一个人在Facebook上的影响所产生的阴谋论进行再造并不能使它们成真，”他说。 “现实是，Facebook的重大决策是由来自不同团队，在不同领域具有不同观点和专业知识的人员提供的意见。提出其他建议是荒谬的。”
 一位致力于Facebook努力保护民主进程并遏制激进主义的诚信研究人员表示，该公司由于出于政治反弹的考虑而拒绝产品变更，从而对用户造成了直接伤害。
    他们在BuzzFeed News看到的内部说明中写道：“出于对潜在的公众和政策利益相关者反应的恐惧，我们有意识地使用户面临诚信风险。”他们在八月辞职。 
那些受琼斯言辞影响最大的人也注意到了。莱尼·波兹纳（Lenny Pozner）的6岁儿子诺亚（Noah）是桑迪·胡克（Sandy Hook）枪击案的最小受害者。他说，在HONR Network（他的大规模伤亡事件幸存者组织）2018年的来信中，该公司已承诺将在处理仇恨和恶作剧方面做得更好。但是，Facebook仍然无法删除有害内容。
 “在某个时刻，”波兹纳告诉BuzzFeed新闻，“扎克伯格必须为自己的角色负责，以使自己的平台武器化，并确保将可笑和危险与事实同等重要。”
  马克·扎克伯格（Mark Zuckerberg）和乔尔·卡普兰（Joel Kaplan）在2019年9月19日在华盛顿特区的国会山办公室与参议员约翰·科宁（R-TX）离开会面后聊天。
  现任和前任雇员表示，卡普兰（Kaplan）与扎克伯格（Zuckerberg）的密切关系使首席执行官在制定高调的内容政策实施决策时，更加重视政治。卡普兰过去四年来努力向特朗普白宫求婚-从他广泛宣传的对最高法院提名人布雷特·卡瓦诺（Brett Kavanaugh）的支持到他在Facebook政策决定中代表右翼有影响力的人士进行的干预-也使他成为民权组织的目标和民主党议员。
 2020年6月，三名民主党参议员在信中询问卡普兰“在Facebook决定关闭并取消对遏制极端主义和两极分化活动的内部努力的优先考虑中起什么作用”。参议员伊丽莎白·沃伦（Elizabeth Warren）呼吁他监督游说活动，该活动花费了数百万美元来影响政客。随着新的总统政府就职以及一系列持续的反托拉斯诉讼，扎克伯格现在必须努力应对这样一个事实，即他的最高政治顾问可能不再是华盛顿特区的资产，而是潜在的债务。
 “我认为DC中的每个人都讨厌Facebook。他们烧毁了每座桥梁。”美国经济自由项目执行董事，乔·拜登总统过渡小组前成员萨拉·米勒说。她说，民主党人对该平台对仇恨言论和错误信息的容忍度感到愤慨，而“拉扯特朗普离开平台”使共和党人对该公司充满了生气。
 米勒补充说：“ Facebook可以大肆宣扬整个政治领域。” 
卡普兰（Kaplan）在2011年加入Facebook领导其DC业务时，他拥有该公司寻求美国权利所需的联系和血统书。他曾担任保守派最高法院法官安东尼·斯卡利亚（Antonin Scalia）的书记员，在2000年佛罗里达州总统大选争议期间参加布鲁克斯兄弟骚乱后，曾担任乔治·W·布什总统下的白宫副参谋长。在2003年的参议院确认听证会上，他在管理和预算办公室任职时，有人质疑卡普兰在这次活动中的角色，该活动试图在佛罗里达州重新计票时停止计票。
 尽管他最初在Facebook上保持低调的公众形象，但Kaplan（首席运营官谢丽尔·桑德伯格的哈佛同学和前男友）因了解共和党决策者和保守的美国人而受到扎克伯格的重视，他们认为首席执行官认为自由派领导团队和雇员所代表的人数不足根据。
    到2014年，他被提升为全球公共政策副总裁。卡普兰（Kaplan）担任该职位时，负责监督该公司在全球的政府关系以及其内容政策团队。这种安排引起了人们的关注，因为其他公司（包括Google和Twitter）通常将公共政策和游说工作与创建和执行内容规则的团队分开。
 唐纳德·特朗普的候选人资格和选举卡普兰做出更有价值的公司。他曾担任扎克伯格的政策顾问，帮助Facebook摆脱谎言，憎恨这位前总统在平台上的变态以及公众对此的愤怒反应。 2015年12月，特朗普在Facebook上发布一条帖子，呼吁穆斯林“全面彻底关闭”进入美国的穆斯林-卡普兰和其他高管中的第一个迫使该公司努力应对当时候选人的种族主义言论，有时甚至是暴力言论。建议Facebook的首席执行官什么都不做。
 据《纽约时报》报道，卡普兰说：“不要戳熊。”他认为，对特朗普的帐户采取行动将引起右翼的强烈反对，并指责该网站限制了言论自由。在随后的五年中，扎克伯格经常会以各种形式重复此论点。
 据现任和前任雇员称，在那段时间里，卡普兰很少在Facebook内部留言板上公开交流或在公司范围内的会议上发表讲话。然而，当他这样做时，他的外表备受争议。
 您在Facebook或其他技术公司工作吗？我们希望收到您的来信。可以访问ryan.mac @ buzzfeed.com，craig.silverman @ buzzfeed.com或通过我们的提示行渠道之一。 
在当时的首席安全官亚历克斯·斯塔莫斯（Alex Stamos）领导的Facebook小组发现俄罗斯在2016年美国总统大选期间及之后干涉该平台的证据后，卡普兰成为领导小组的成员，该小组反对公开披露克里姆林宫的影响力活动的全部范围。当公司最终在2017年10月公开发布有关它的更多信息时，是Kaplan而非Stamos在内部市政厅回答了员工的问题。
 斯塔莫斯说：“他们本来可以派我的。”他后来因与俄罗斯干涉有关的分歧而离开了公司。 “向[VP]展示[俄罗斯战役的证据]的人是我。”
 然而，正是卡普兰（Kaplan）出席卡瓦诺（Kavanaugh）2018年9月的参议院确认听证会，才使他成为全国关注的焦点。坐在被提名人的身后，他在活动的电视转播中可见。员工大怒；他们认为卡普兰的出席使Facebook看起来像是在支持被提名人，同时驳回了针对他的性侵犯指控。
  乔尔·卡普兰（Joel Kaplan）于2018年9月27日在华盛顿特区举行的参议院司法委员会确认听证会上与最高法院提名候选人布雷特·卡瓦诺（Brett Kavanaugh）的家人，朋友和支持者坐在一起。
  卡普兰随后通过电视会议在公司范围内的会议上解决了这一事件，愤怒的工人感到他在相机上的出现是故意的，使他提出了疑问。但是在他自己的团队内部，他面临着一些最激烈的批评。在那个秋天的Facebook公共政策团队会议上，一位长期经理含泪地指责他对同性攻击幸存者缺乏同情。
 “认识一个人并不重要；两名参加会议的人士说，这并不意味着他们没有按照别人所说的去做。随后，她写了一篇公共博客文章，讲述了自己遭受性侵犯的经历，并于一年后离开了公司。
 这些都没有改变卡普兰在扎克伯格的地位。该首席执行官于2019年9月去了华盛顿特区，并在一次旅行中被卡普兰（Kaplan）牧养，其中包括与特朗普会晤。卡普兰对特朗普白宫保持友好，特朗普白宫曾一度认为他经营管理和预算办公室。 
今年五月，当扎克伯格决定不碰特朗普在乔治·弗洛伊德（George Floyd）抗议期间的“抢劫开始，枪击事件开始”的煽动时，工人们变得愤怒了。在随后的公司范围内的会议上，来自员工的最强烈的疑问之一直接叫出Kaplan。问题被问到：“许多人认为乔尔·卡普兰（Joel Kaplan）对我们的决定有太多权力。”要求副总统解释他的角色和价值观。
 扎克伯格似乎对这个问题感到不满，并质疑任何人都可能影响公司做出决定的“严格”过程的观点。首席执行官认为，多样性意味着要考虑所有政治观点。
 扎克伯格说：“这基本上是根据乔尔是共和党人这一事实来询问乔尔是可以担任这个角色还是可以扮演这个角色……我不得不说，我觉得这条疑问线非常令人困扰。”说，无视这个问题。 “如果我们想切实做好为人民服务的工作，（我们必须）考虑到对不同事物有不同的看法。”
 Facebook员工表示，扎克伯格仍然坚决支持卡普兰，但内部压力正在逐渐减少，以减少公共政策团队的影响力。 Facebook公民诚信团队负责人Samidh Chakrabarti在6月的一份内部说明中写道，同事“感到压力，以确保他们的建议与决策者的利益保持一致”，哀叹在履行团队职责：制止滥用行为时，在平衡这些利益方面存在困难。和选举干扰平台。据《新闻》报道，选举后不久，公民诚信小组解散。
 Chakrabarti说：“他们将此归因于组织激励，即内容政策和公共政策团队具有共同的根基。” “只要是这种情况，我们将过早将监管利益置于社区保护之上。”
 现在担任斯坦福互联网天文台负责人的斯塔莫斯说，政策小组的结构将始终以当前形式提出问题。
 他说：“您不希望平台政策制定者向负责使政府中的人们感到高兴的人汇报。” “乔尔来自布什白宫，政府关系在言论要求上没有中立立场。” 
8月，负责监督新闻源的Facebook产品经理向同事们介绍了该公司为2020年美国大选所做的准备。
 内部研究表明，脸书上的人们在政治讨论小组中被两极分化，这也为错误信息和仇恨提供了温床。为了解决这个问题，负责保护选举完整性的Facebook员工建议该平台停止在名为“您应该加入的团体”的模块中推荐此类团体。
  产品经理在Facebook内部留言板上写道：“尽管“产品推荐”将改善公民过滤器的实施，但会在[2020年美国大选期间]在政治生态系统中引起轰动。” “我们决定在选举结束之前不做任何改变。”
 该社交网络最终在11月大选前几周暂停了政治团体的建议，直到1月6日国会大厦起义后才将其永久删除。现任和前雇员表示，Facebook决定不理会其诚信团队。指导和最初不让团体提出建议的情况，充分说明了政治计算经常如何压制可能使虚假信息和激进主义蒙混过关的公司举措。
 在关于小组建议的同一更新中，产品经理还解释了领导者由于潜在的政治忧虑而决定不对称为In Feed Recommendations（IFR）的功能进行更改的决定。 IFR旨在将帖子从他们不关注的帐户插入人们的供稿中，旨在促进新的人脉或兴趣。例如，如果某人关注了堪萨斯城酋长之类的橄榄球队的Facebook页面，那么IFR可能会将NFL的帖子添加到其供稿中，即使该人没有关注NFL。
 IFR不应做的一件事就是推荐政治内容。但是那年春天的早些时候，Facebook用户开始抱怨说，尽管他们从未参与过这类内容，但他们在新闻摘要中看到了包括本·夏皮罗（Ben Shapiro）这样的保守人物的帖子。
 当内部对该问题进行标记时，Facebook的内容政策小组警告说，删除有关政治内容的此类建议可能会减少这些页面的参与度和访问量，并可能引起发布者的投诉。新闻提要产品经理和政策小组成员在8月Facebook内部留言板上的帖子中重申了这一观点。 
“这些生产者的分布明显下降（通过交通洞察力提出建议）可能导致备受瞩目的升级，其中可能包括在2020年美国大选期间对某些政治实体实施影子禁令和/或FB偏见的指控，”他们解释了。影子禁止或在不通知页面所有者的情况下限制页面发行量是右翼人士针对社交媒体平台提出的普遍指控。
    前核心数据科学家在备忘录中指出，在2020年全年，“对强大的政治行为者的恐惧”成为关键的公共政策团队合理化，以便在美国总统大选前放弃对潜在违法内容采取的行动或推出产品变更。 。他们还说，他们已经看到“有十个提议被用来衡量或精简News Feed内容客观质量的提议，因为……它们在美国政治领域产生了不成比例的影响，通常会更加损害保守的内容。”
 这位数据科学家曾在公司工作了五年以上，然后于去年年底离职，他指出，尽管自2016年以来取得了长足进步，但News Feed的政治内容状况“仍然普遍被认为是不好的”。他们补充说，根据Facebook的数据，每100篇关于美国政治的内容的观点中有1篇是关于某种恶作剧的，而对政治材料的大多数观点都是在党派职位上发表的。数据科学家说，如果该公司被认为是“敏感的”或可能进行报复的话，它将继续给已知的虚假和误导性信息传播者以通行证。
 他们写道：“在美国，干预似乎几乎完全是代表保守的出版商，”这归因于政治压力或不愿让敏感的出版商和知名用户感到不安。
 正如BuzzFeed News去年夏天报道的那样，包括Kaplan在内的Facebook政策小组成员代表右翼人物和诸如Charlie Kirk，Breitzart和Prager University之类的出版物进行干预，在某些情况下，要求消除对其页面上的误报或帐户。根据Facebook的第三方事实检查人员的建议，应用罢工会导致一系列的处罚，从降低其帖子分发的距离到删除页面或帐户的处罚。
 卡普兰的其他干预措施都有据可查。 2018年，《华尔街日报》（Wall Street Journal）透露他帮助杀死了一个将具有政治分歧的美国人联系起来的项目。该报导说，卡普兰曾反对“在接受Facebook内部研究的简报时发现，向右倾斜的用户比向左倾斜的用户更倾向于两极化，或者更少地暴露于不同的观点。”去年，《纽约时报》报道了

In April 2019, Facebook was preparing to ban one of the internet’s most notorious spreaders of misinformation and hate, Infowars founder Alex Jones. Then CEO Mark Zuckerberg personally intervened.
 Jones had  gained infamy for claiming that the 2012 Sandy Hook elementary school massacre was a “giant hoax,” and that the teenage survivors of the 2018 Parkland shooting were “crisis actors.” But Facebook had found that he was also relentlessly spreading hate against various groups, including Muslims and trans people. That behavior qualified him for expulsion from the social network under  the company's policies for "dangerous individuals and organizations," which required Facebook to also remove any content that expressed “praise or support” for them.
 But Zuckerberg didn’t consider the Infowars founder to be a hate figure, according to a person familiar with the decision, so he overruled his own internal experts and opened a gaping loophole: Facebook would permanently ban Jones and his company — but would not touch posts of praise and support for them from other Facebook users. This meant that Jones’ legions of followers could continue to share his lies across the world’s largest social network.
 "Mark personally didn’t like the punishment, so he changed the rules,” a former policy employee told BuzzFeed News, noting that the original rule had already been in use and represented the product of untold hours of work between multiple teams and experts.
    “That was the first time I experienced having to create a new category of policy to fit what Zuckerberg wanted. It's somewhat demoralizing when we have established a policy and it’s gone through rigorous cycles. Like, what the fuck is that for?” said a second former policy employee who, like the first, asked not to be named so they could speak about internal matters.
 “Mark called for a more nuanced policy and enforcement strategy,” Facebook spokesperson Andy Stone said of the Alex Jones decision, which also affected the  bans of other extremist figures.
 Zuckerberg’s “more nuanced policy” set off a cascading effect, the two former employees said, which delayed the company’s efforts to remove right wing militant organizations such as the Oath Keepers, which were involved the Jan. 6 insurrection at the US Capitol. It is also a case study in Facebook’s willingness to change its rules to placate America’s right wing and avoid political backlash.
 Internal documents obtained by BuzzFeed News and interviews with 14 current and former employees show how the company’s policy team — guided by Joel Kaplan, the vice president of global public policy, and Zuckerberg’s whims — has exerted outsize influence while obstructing content moderation decisions, stymieing product rollouts, and intervening on behalf of popular conservative figures who have violated Facebook’s rules.
 In December, a former core data scientist wrote a memo titled, “Political Influences on Content Policy.” Seen by BuzzFeed News, the memo stated that Kaplan’s policy team “regularly protects powerful constituencies” and listed several examples, including: removing penalties for misinformation from right-wing pages, blunting attempts to improve content quality in News Feed, and briefly blocking a proposal to stop recommending political groups ahead of the US election.
 Since the November vote, at least six Facebook employees have resigned with farewell posts that have called out leadership’s failures to heed its own experts on misinformation and hate speech. Four departing employees explicitly cited the policy organization as an impediment to their work and called for a reorganization so that the public policy team, which oversees lobbying and government relations, and the content policy team, which sets and enforces the platform’s rules, would not both report to Kaplan.
 Facebook declined to make Kaplan or other executives available for an interview. Stone, the company spokesperson, dismissed concerns about the vice president’s influence.
 “Recycling the same warmed over conspiracy theories about the influence of one person at Facebook doesn’t make them true,” he said. “The reality is big decisions at Facebook are made with input from people across different teams who have different perspectives and expertise in different areas. To suggest otherwise is absurd.”
 An integrity researcher who worked on Facebook’s efforts to protect the democratic process and rein in radicalization said the company caused direct harm to users by rejecting product changes due to concerns of political backlash.
    “Out of fears over  potential public and policy stakeholder responses, we are  knowingly exposing users to risks of integrity,” they wrote in an internal note seen by BuzzFeed News. They quit in August.
 Those most affected by Jones’ rhetoric have taken notice, too. Lenny Pozner, whose 6-year-old son Noah was the youngest victim of the Sandy Hook shooting, called the revelation that Zuckerberg weakened penalties facing the Infowars founder “disheartening, but not surprising.” He said the company had made a promise to do better in dealing with hate and hoaxes following  a 2018 letter from HONR Network, his organization for survivors of mass casualty events. Yet Facebook continues to fail to remove harmful content.
 “At some point,” Pozner told BuzzFeed News, “Zuckerberg has to be held responsible for his role in allowing his platform to be weaponized and for ensuring that the ludicrous and the dangerous are given equal importance as the factual.”
  Mark Zuckerberg and Joel Kaplan chat after leaving a meeting with Sen. John Cornyn (R-TX) in his office on Capitol Hill on September 19, 2019 in Washington, DC.
  Kaplan’s close relationship with Zuckerberg has led the CEO to weigh politics more heavily when making high-profile content policy enforcement decisions, current and former employees said. Kaplan’s efforts to court the Trump White House over the past four years — from his widely publicized support for Supreme Court nominee Brett Kavanaugh to  his interventions on behalf of right-wing influencers in Facebook policy decisions — have also made him a target for civil rights groups and Democratic lawmakers.
 In June 2020,  three Democratic senators asked in a letter what role Kaplan played “in Facebook’s decision to shut down and de-prioritize internal efforts to contain extremist and hyperpolarizing activity.”  Sen. Elizabeth Warren called him out for overseeing a lobbying effort that spends millions of dollars to influence politicians. With a new presidential administration in place and a spate of ongoing antitrust lawsuits, Zuckerberg must now grapple with the fact that his top political adviser may no longer be a Washington, DC asset but a potential liability.
 “I think that everybody in DC hates Facebook. They have burned every bridge,” said Sarah Miller, executive director of the American Economic Liberties Project and a former member of Joe Biden’s presidential transition team. Democrats are incensed with the platform’s tolerance of hate speech and misinformation, while “pulling Trump off the platform” has brought new life to Republican gripes with the company, she said.
 “Facebook has fires to put out all across the political spectrum,” Miller added.
    When Kaplan joined Facebook to lead its DC operation in 2011, he had the connections and pedigree the company needed to court the American right. A former clerk for conservative Supreme Court Justice Antonin Scalia, he served as a White House deputy chief of staff under President George W. Bush after participating in the  Brooks Brothers riot during the 2000 Florida presidential election dispute. During a Senate confirmation hearing in 2003 for a post with the Office of Management and Budget, Kaplan was  questioned about his role in the event, which sought to stop the tallying of votes during the Florida recount.
 Though he initially maintained a low public profile at Facebook, Kaplan — COO Sheryl Sandberg’s Harvard classmate and former boyfriend — was valued by Zuckerberg for understanding of GOP policymakers and conservative Americans, who the CEO believed were underrepresented by a liberal-leaning leadership team and employee base.
    By 2014, he’d been promoted to vice president of global public policy. In that role, Kaplan oversaw the company’s government relations around the world as well as its content policy team. That arrangement raised eyebrows, as other companies, including Google and Twitter, typically keep public policy and lobbying efforts separate from teams that create and enforce content rules.
 The candidacy and election of Donald Trump made Kaplan even more valuable to the company. He served as Zuckerberg’s policy consigliere, helping Facebook navigate the sea of lies and hate the former president conjured on the platform as well as the outraged public response to it. In December 2015, following  a Facebook post from Trump calling for a “total and complete shutdown” of Muslims entering the US — the first of many that forced the company to grapple with the then-candidate’s racist and sometimes violent rhetoric — Kaplan and other executives advised Facebook’s CEO to do nothing.
 “Don’t poke the bear,” Kaplan said,  according to the New York Times, arguing that taking action against Trump’s account would invite a right-wing backlash and accusations that the site was limiting free speech. It’s an argument he’d repeat in various forms over the ensuing five years, with Zuckerberg often in agreement.
 During that time, Kaplan rarely communicated openly on Facebook’s internal message boards or spoke at companywide meetings, according to current and former employees. When he did, however, his appearances were clouded in controversy.
 Do you work at Facebook or another technology company? We'd love to hear from you. Reach out to  ryan.mac@buzzfeed.com,  craig.silverman@buzzfeed.com,  or  via one of our   tip line channels .
 After a Facebook team led by then–chief security officer Alex Stamos found evidence of Russian interference on the platform during and after the 2016 US presidential election, Kaplan was part of a leadership group that argued against disclosing the full extent of the Kremlin’s influence operation. When the company did end up publicly releasing further information about it in October 2017, it was Kaplan, not Stamos, who answered employee questions during an internal town hall.
 “They could have sent me,” said Stamos, who  subsequently left the company over disagreements related to Russian interference. “The person who was presenting [evidence of the Russian campaign] to VPs was me.”
 It was Kaplan’s appearance at Kavanaugh’s September 2018 Senate confirmation hearings, however, that pushed him into the national spotlight. Sitting behind the nominee, he was visible in TV coverage of the event. Employees were furious; they believed Kaplan's attendance made it look like Facebook supported the nominee, while dismissing the allegations of sexual assault against him.
  Joel Kaplan sits with family members, friends, and supporters of Supreme Court nominee Brett Kavanaugh during his testimony before a Senate Judiciary Committee confirmation hearing in Washington, DC, on September 27, 2018.
  Kaplan subsequently addressed the incident at a companywide meeting via videoconference, where angry workers, who felt his on-camera appearance was intentional, hammered him with questions. But it was inside his own team that he faced some of the fiercest criticism. During a Facebook public policy team meeting that fall, a longtime manager tearfully accused him of lacking empathy for survivors of sexual assault.
 “It doesn’t matter how well you know someone; it doesn’t mean they didn’t do what somebody said they did,” the policy manager said, according to two people in attendance. She subsequently wrote a public blog post about her experience of being sexually assaulted and left the company a year later.
 None of this changed Kaplan’s standing with Zuckerberg. The CEO  went to DC in September 2019 and was shepherded around by Kaplan on a trip that included a meeting with Trump. Kaplan remained friendly with the Trump White House, which at one point  considered him to run the Office of Management and Budget.
    In May, when Zuckerberg decided to not touch Trump’s “when the looting starts, the shooting starts” incitement during the  George Floyd protests,  workers became incensed. At a subsequent companywide meeting, one of the most upvoted questions from employees directly called Kaplan out. “Many people feel that Joel Kaplan has too much power over our decisions,” the question read, asking that the vice president explain his role and values.
 Zuckerberg seemed irked by the question and  disputed the notion that any one person could influence the “rigorous” process by which the company made decisions. Diversity, the CEO argued, means taking into account all political views.
 “That basically asked whether Joel can be in this role, or can be doing this role, on the basis of the fact that he is a Republican … and I have to say that I find that line of questioning to be very troubling,” Zuckerberg said, ignoring the question. “If we want to actually do a good job of serving people, [we have to take] into account that there are different views on different things.”
 Facebook employees said Zuckerberg remains stalwart in his support for Kaplan, but internal pressure is building to reduce the public policy team’s influence. Colleagues “feel pressure to ensure their recommendations align with the interests of policymakers,” Samidh Chakrabarti, head of Facebook’s civic integrity team, wrote in an internal note in June, bemoaning the difficulty of balancing such interests while delivering on the team’s mandate: stopping abuse and election interference on the platform. The civic integrity team was disbanded shortly after the election, as reported by  the Information.
 “They attribute this to the organizational incentives of having the content policy and public policy teams share a common root,” Chakrabarti said. “As long as this is the case, we will be prematurely prioritizing regulatory interests over community protection.”
 Stamos, who is now head of the Stanford Internet Observatory, said the policy team’s structure will always present a problem in its current form.
 “You don’t want platform policy people reporting to someone who’s in charge of keeping people in government happy,” he said. “Joel comes from the Bush White House, and government relations does not have a neutral position on speech requests.”
    In August, a Facebook product manager who oversees the News Feed updated his colleagues on the company’s preparations for the 2020 US election.
 Internal  research had shown that people on Facebook were being polarized on the site in political discussion groups, which were also breeding grounds for misinformation and hate. To combat this, Facebook employees who were tasked with protecting election integrity proposed the platform stop recommending such groups in a module called “Groups You Should Join.”
  “Although the Product recommendation would have improved implementation of the civic filter, it would have created thrash in the political ecosystem during [ the 2020 US election,]” the product manager wrote on Facebook's internal message board. “We have decided to not make any changes until the election is over.”
 The social network eventually  paused political group recommendations — just weeks before the November election — and removed them permanently only after the Capitol insurrection on Jan. 6. Current and former employees said Facebook's decision to ignore its integrity team's guidance and initially leave group recommendations untouched exemplifies how political calculations often quashed company initiatives that could have blunted misinformation and radicalization.
 In that same update about group recommendations, the product manager also explained how leaders decided against making changes to a feature called In Feed Recommendations (IFR) due to potential political worries. Designed to insert posts into people’s feeds from accounts they don’t follow, IFR was intended to foster new connections or interests. For example, if a person followed the Facebook page for a football team like the Kansas City Chiefs, IFR might add a post from the NFL to their feed, even if that person didn’t follow the NFL.
 One thing IFR was not supposed to do was recommend political content. But earlier that spring, Facebook users began complaining that they were seeing posts from conservative personalities including Ben Shapiro in their News Feeds even though they had never engaged with that type of content.
 When the issue was flagged internally, Facebook’s content policy team warned that removing such suggestions for political content could reduce those pages’ engagement and traffic, and possibly inspire complaints from publishers. A News Feed product manager and a policy team member reiterated this argument in an August post to Facebook’s internal message board.
 “A noticeable drop in distribution for these producers (via traffic insights for recommendations) is likely to result in high-profile escalations that could include accusations of shadow-banning and/or FB bias against certain political entities during the US 2020 election cycle,” they explained. Shadow-banning, or the limiting of a page’s circulation without informing its owners, is a common accusation leveled by right-wing personalities against social media platforms.
    Throughout 2020, the “fear of antagonizing powerful political actors,” as the former core data scientist put it in their memo, became a key public policy team rationalization for forgoing action on potentially violative content or rolling out product changes ahead of the US presidential election. They also said they had seen “a dozen proposals to measure the objective quality of content on News Feed diluted or killed because … they have a disproportionate impact across the US political spectrum, typically harming conservative content more.”
 The data scientist, who spent more than five years at the company before leaving late last year, noted that while strides had been made since 2016, the state of political content on News Feed was “still generally agreed to be bad.” According to Facebook data, they added, 1 of every 100 views on content about US politics was for some type of hoax, while the majority of views for political materials were on partisan posts. Yet the company continued to give known spreaders of false and misleading information a pass if they were deemed “‘sensitive’ or likely to retaliate,” the data scientist said.
 “In the US it appears that interventions have been almost exclusively on behalf of conservative publishers,” they wrote, attributing this to political pressure or a reluctance to upset sensitive publishers and high-profile users.
 As  BuzzFeed News reported last summer, members of Facebook’s policy team — including Kaplan — intervened on behalf of right-wing figures and publications such as Charlie Kirk,  Breitbart, and  Prager University, in some cases pushing for the removal of misinformation strikes against their pages or accounts. Strikes, which are applied at the recommendation of Facebook’s third-party fact-checkers, can result in a range of penalties, from a decrease in how far their posts are distributed to the removal of the page or account.
 Kaplan’s other interventions are well documented. In 2018, the Wall Street Journal  revealed that he helped kill a project to connect Americans who have political differences. The paper said Kaplan had objected “when briefed on internal Facebook research that found right-leaning users tended to be more polarized, or less exposed to different points of view, than those on the left.” Last year,  the New York Times reported that polic

跳转到导航跳转到搜索Canon Cat是佳能公司于1987年发布的专用于任务的台式计算机，价格为1,495美元。从表面上看，它与1970年代末至1980年代初流行的专用文字处理器没有什么不同，但它的功能强大得多，并结合了许多独特的数据处理思想。
   佳能猫最初是由杰夫·拉斯金（Jef Raskin）创立的，后者是苹果公司Macintosh项目的创始人。 1982年离开公司后，他开始设计一种新计算机，使其更接近于最初的廉价，实用型计算机的构想。 1987年，BYTE将Cat描述为Macintosh的精神继承者。 [1]它具有文本用户界面，不使用任何指针（鼠标），图标或图形。所有数据都被视为很长的＆＃34; stream＆＃34;分为几页的文本。 Cat不使用传统的命令行界面或菜单系统，而是使用其特殊的键盘，通过按住＆＃34; Use Front＆＃34;来激活命令。键，然后按另一个键。 Cat还使用特殊的“飞跃键”，当按住该键时，用户可以逐步搜索字符串。
  该机器的硬件包括一个9英寸（229毫米）的黑白显示器，一个3½英寸的256 KB软盘驱动器和一个IBM Selectric兼容键盘。它使用以5 MHz运行的Motorola 68000 CPU（如Macintosh，Lisa，Atari ST和Amiga），具有256 KB的RAM和内部300/1200 bit / s调制解调器。设置和用户首选项数据存储在8 KB的非易失性（电池备份）RAM中。 Cat的I / O接口阵列包括一个Centronics并行端口，一个RS-232C串行端口（DB-25）和两个用于调制解调器环路的RJ11电话插孔。系统的总重量为17磅（7.7千克）。
 256 KB的ROM中内置了广泛的应用软件：标准的办公套件程序，通信，一个90,000个单词拼写词典以及用于Forth和汇编语言的用户编程工具链。
 Cat是一台纯文本机器，包括ROM中的图形例程以及从未使用过的鼠标或其他定点设备的连接器。 [2]
 由Raskin发起的一个名为Archy的已停产软件项目，旨在为当前的计算系统开发类似但功能更强大的系统。该项目旨在最终替代当前的软件用户界面。
    ^ Shapiro，Ezra（1987年10月）。 ＆＃34; Macintosh的精神继承人＆＃34;。字节。 p。 121。
note_en:
Jump to navigation  Jump to search  Canon Cat is a task-dedicated desktop  computer released by  Canon Inc. in 1987 at the price of  U.S. $1,495. On the surface, it was not unlike dedicated  word processors popular in the late 1970s to early 1980s, but it was far more powerful, and incorporated many unique ideas for data manipulation.
   Canon Cat is primarily the creation of  Jef Raskin, the originator of the  Macintosh project at  Apple. After leaving the company in 1982, he began designing a new computer closer to his original vision of an inexpensive, utilitarian "people's computer";   BYTE in 1987 described the Cat as "a  spiritual heir to the Macintosh".  [1] It features a  text user interface, not making use of any pointer ( mouse),  icons, or  graphics. All data are seen as a long "stream" of text broken into several pages. Instead of using a traditional  command line interface or  menu system, the Cat makes use of its special  keyboard, with commands being activated by holding down a "Use Front" key and pressing another key. The Cat also uses special "Leap keys", which, when held down, allows the user to  incrementally search for  strings of characters.
  The machine's hardware consists of a 9-inch (229 mm) black-and-white  monitor, a single 3½-inch 256  KB  floppy disk drive, and an  IBM Selectric–compatible  keyboard. It uses a  Motorola 68000  CPU (like the Macintosh,  Lisa,  Atari ST, and  Amiga) running at 5  MHz, has 256 KB of  RAM, and an internal 300/1200 bit/s  modem. Setup and user preference data are stored in 8 KB of non-volatile (battery backed-up) RAM. The Cat's array of  I/O interfaces encompasses one  Centronics  parallel port, one  RS-232C  serial port ( DB-25), and two  RJ11 telephone jacks for the modem loop. The total weight of the system is 17 pounds (7.7 kg).
 An extensive range of application software is built into 256 KB of  ROM: standard  office suite programs, communications, a 90,000 word spelling dictionary, and user programming  toolchains for  Forth and  assembly language.
 A text-only machine, the Cat includes graphics routines in ROM as well as connectors for a  mouse or other  pointing device that are never used.  [2]
 There is a defunct software project called  Archy, initiated by Raskin, to develop a similar yet even more capable system for current computing systems. The project was designed to eventually replace current software  user interfaces.
    ^   Shapiro, Ezra (October 1987).  "A Spiritual Heir to the Macintosh".  BYTE. p. 121 .

大卫：所以我今天要与标准电子书的创始人兼总编辑亚历克斯·卡巴尔（Alex Cabal）进行交流，这是一个自愿组织，致力于制作精美，格式正确的经典文学作品电子书，这就是其背后的全部哲学。组织。
 首先，关于您自己的背景知识，Alex。你在哪儿长大的？您如何来到今天的位置？
 亚历克斯：恩，我在旧金山湾区长大，在那里度过了我的青春，然后我在洛杉矶的加利福尼亚大学上学。我学习计算机科学和英语。我学。我都做了，都辅修了英语。大学毕业后，我从事计算机程序员，网络开发人员的工作，但我不太喜欢这种9到5的情况，所以我决定开办自己的公司，即Scribophile。那是我的主要收入来源。
 之后，我趁机进行了远程工作。这可能是13或14年前，所以它不像今天那样普遍，因此我是最早的人之一-我想您可以称呼他们为数字游牧民，现在您一直在听到。在14年前的13、13时，我是当时这样做的少数人之一，所以我花了很多时间在欧洲，澳大利亚，新西兰，亚洲和其他地方旅行。南美洲。一旦我觉得自己做完了，就来到了芝加哥，那里有很多家庭居住。我决定在那里定居。所以我现在在芝加哥已经有一段时间了。
  大卫：好的。那么，是什么让您想到了启动标准电子书的想法呢？稍后我们将讨论有关Scribophile的问题。但是，启动标准电子书的动力是什么（我认为您将其描述为一种爱好）？
 亚历克斯：是的，现在基本上是一种爱好。我的意思是，我们只是在最近才开始接受捐款，但是这并不意味着会有大量的钱进来，因此肯定仍处于业余爱好水平。之所以开始，是因为我旅行时在德国住了一段时间（一两年），在那儿很难找到英语书籍。您必须在大城市，而我不在大城市。
 因此，我当时拥有电子阅读器。我认为当时是个角落。我开始从Amazon商店，Nook商店购买书籍，而且格式对所有书籍都非常糟糕。再说一次，这大概是八年前，大概是十年前，当时，即使是对于主要出版商，他们出售的许多后背目录书也只是不好的OCR –光学字符识别–转录，所以它们会充满错别字和错误，所以我坐在那里真是疯了，因为我真的为这本书付出了很多钱，而这本书的质量并不是很好。您永远不会在纸上打印。太尴尬了。 
因此，我开始将它们收集到自己的图书馆中，而不是分发给我，只是为了我自己，因为我买了这些书，并可能为自己保留一个不错的版本，然后随着时间的推移，我决定从古腾堡计划中下载一些东西，因为每个人我知道这是收集大量经典文学和其他公共领域文学的好地方，因此，我开始下载这些电子书并将其转移到我的电子阅读器中。而且我意识到，尽管它们还没有达到原始OCR的水平，但就错别字和类似内容而言，它们的质量还是相当不错的，但是显然，它们并未针对电子阅读器的功能进行格式化。因此，您在阅读这些书籍时，即使您在应该像印刷书籍一样的电子墨水设备上阅读它们，印刷形式也更像是网页而不是印刷书籍。因此，我尽了最大努力为自己的图书馆编辑这些商业电子书，这是我的出发点。我对自己说：为什么我不只是为这些公共领域的书做这个，这样做的好处是我可以再把它们赠予别人？我可以建立一个网站并进行分发，然后每个人都可以享受自己为自己制作的这些制作精良的书，但是，实际上，任何人都会有兴趣获得按这种标准制作的副本。
 因此，这就是我起步的方式，我已经为自己做了一年，只是断断续续，然后我决定坐下来建立网站并开始正式分发它们。所以我建立了网站，大概我想说是六年前，五年前之类的。
 并在Hacker News上获得了好评，带来了大量的访问量。它遍及Reddit以及大量博客和新闻媒体。每个人似乎都真的很喜欢这个项目，从那时起，我只是决定从中受益，然后在这里我们经历了很多年，后来有数百本电子书，后来又有来自世界各地的数百位撰稿人，很大，我会说人们喜欢的成功，所以我为此感到自豪。
  大卫：我认为您有很多值得骄傲的地方。您即将在该网站上阅读近500本书。从某种意义上说，这很有趣。它混合了不同来源，不同体裁的书籍。它们都是公共领域的书籍。但是，您如何决定要开始制作哪些书籍，以及您决定作为官方标准电子书作品开始制作的前几本书呢？
 亚历克斯：嗯，原始书籍基本上是我想亲自阅读的。因此，在我自己的个人喜好中，所有事情都是有趣的。我不记得哪个是第一个，但最早的是德古拉和Epictetus撰写的《 Enchiridion》，如今我们可能会把它编入汇编，因为它很短，但是因为它是第一个。我们制作的书籍，现在只是靠它自己。
    其他一些是火星公主。那是早期的之一。像这样的书，所以到处都是。涉及到哲学和经典小说，还有任何现代小说-我个人非常喜欢科幻小说-我们可以适应的仍然是公共领域。
 我希望人们不会将它们称为经典，因为情况并非总是如此。我的意思是，我们确实确实有很多经典著作，它们是您在学校可能会读的书，或者每个人在某个时候都读过的书，因此它们具有某种文化影响力。我们做的书都是公共领域的，但是它们可能会更新很多，因此我们有许多50年代甚至60年代的科幻书籍，由于各种原因进入了美国公共领域。 
那里还有一些其他现代化的东西，也有很多稀缺的东西，我根本不会叫经典。只是因为他们老了并不意味着他们是经典。我的意思是，有哲学书籍，或其他小说书籍因任何原因而变得模糊，但仍然非常有趣。所以我们在那里有各种各样的书籍，我们想要的名单最初是我想读书的书籍，所以我的希望是那个想要的清单是别人对我做的努力工作，然后我可以坐下来读书图书。但正如我一直在努力这个项目的那样，人们将这些书籍从列表中赶出，而不是我可以阅读它们，然后我也在阅读我自己的书籍，我为该项目生产。所以我意识到我永远不会有时间阅读任何这些书籍，所以 - 
  大卫：嗯，读整个目录是很难掌握的舞台，这是肯定的。
 亚历克斯：对，所以这几乎是我想到这一点的问题。
  大卫：所以我猜这本书被选中工作 - 它真的取决于个体生产者不是吗？ - 他们的个人品味被反映在目录中。
  所以标准电子书有一个系列的一个系列政策，这是我们基本上接受各种小说。我们尽力维持较短的工作的汇编，所以与像H. P. Loveraft这样的人一起写了很多短暂的恐怖故事，而不是将每个人都作为个人电子书，我们只是将它们编译成一个所有这一简短小说的综合。所以你不必在下载数百个文件时争抢。然后我们接受了一些我叫永恒的非小说，这是哲学和一些自传，一套有限的传记，那种东西。我想这就是关于它的。然后我们接受戏剧，那种东西。在小说类型下它的堕落。
 但正如你所说，基本上是生产者个人想要生产的东西。这就是他们的工作原因，因为他们必须阅读它，所以他们必须有兴趣阅读它，并且很多时候都意味着犯下一本非常长的书。如果他们不感兴趣，那么为什么要打扰？
  大卫：是的，那是对的。嗯，刚刚完成了英国文学中最长的作品之一，如果没有参加这个项目，我将永远不会阅读它。 
亚历克斯：是的，你做到了。我查看了网站上的字数统计，它的长度几乎是《战争与和平》的两倍。
     戴维：是的，这是一本很长的书，却是一本书。
   大卫：我很高兴完成它，但我也很高兴阅读它。这是一本非常好的书。无论如何，如果不是因为这个项目，我认为我不会读它。
 但是我猜想的另一件事是像我这样的制作人确实会以这种方式发现书籍。在进入标准电子书之前，我认为我不会读过任何乔治·艾略特。我正在浏览《英语百佳小说》的其中一份监护人名单，然后看到乔治·埃利奥特（George Eliot）的《中间人》。我认为“听起来像是，人们认为它还不错”，所以我做到了。这就是我发现乔治·艾略特（George Eliot）的方式。我真的很高兴。她是一位了不起的作家。那挺好的。
 亚历克斯：实际上，我没有太多机会阅读乔治·艾略特（George Eliot），因此在某些时候，Middlemarch肯定是在通缉名单上的，但现在仍在我的个人阅读清单上。最终，一旦我了解了，是吗？
  大卫：好的，是的。好吧，我会选择那儿的，而不是牙线磨坊，我认为这有点平淡，但是Middlemarch非常好。
 现在，我想问一问书的外观，这是一个非常独特的外观，因为您强调制作出真正好看的封面的想法，并且采用了某种方法。你想谈谈吗？书的外观，以及您如何决定和维护该政策的方式。 
亚历克斯：好的。我们所有的封面均来自公共领域的艺术品。通常喜欢美术，油画风格的美术。当我为自己制作这些电子书时，我一直认为封面显然是一本书的重要组成部分。从Gutenberg项目下载的许多书（这是我最初从那里下载的书）都具有这些自动生成的封面，我认为这些封面并不十分吸引人。它们只是这些随机的几何形状和非常鲜艳的扎染颜色中的一种，然后作者和书名在那里，对位置或布局没有太多的想法。
 无论如何，我心想，也许我们可以做得更好，如果我们已经在研究公共领域的书籍，为什么不尝试找到与该主题相匹配的公共领域的艺术品呢？封面的一般样式。制作人必须找到一幅公共领域的美术绘画，该绘画以某种方式反映了该书的内容，并且只是其封面。然后我们有一个非常简单的黑色方块，标题和作者在其下，所有书籍的位置相同。因此，就像项目的名称一样，它成为一种标准，您可以在其中看到封面，就像一幅美术绘画，然后在书本和作者的名字中看到带有特定字体的黑色正方形。然后您可以说，哦，可能是一本标准电子书。
 我的意思是说这没有什么大的科学。我只是坐了一天，说这些看起来不错。我担任网页设计师已有很长时间了，我不会说我是一个特别有天赋的人。我不是很喜欢平面设计领域，但是我花了很多时间在这些网站上工作，所以我认为其中一部分来自那里，您只是在关注页边距和填充以及什么样的外观才有吸引力什么没有。人们似乎很喜欢被子–
  大卫：是的，绝对。对他们来说肯定有非常独特和吸引人的外观。
 您制作，发行或献给公共领域的每一本书，从一开始就显然是一种哲学。那是因为材料始于公共领域吗？其他组织或其他人可能会认为，“哦，我可以通过这样做来赚钱”，但是您显然认为这不是方法。
 亚历克斯：是的，老实说，我认为公共领域的书中没有很多钱。如果您在亚马逊上搜索Dracula，则有人将其拼凑成一本电子书，大约有5,000个cr脚的版本，而这些版本都是免费的，或约为0.99美元。
 因此，公共领域电子书的收入不菲。我要说的是公共领域的印刷书籍，例如，可能有一些市场（如Dover）从中获利。 
亚历克斯：您可以制作教育版或带注释的版本，如企鹅经典之类，诸如此类，这是一个不同的故事，但是就电子书而言，我从没想过里面会有钱，而且没有是。
 就公共领域的奉献精神而言，这本书本身的内容已经在美国的公共领域，因此我们的奉献精神并没有发生任何变化。即使我们坐下来说，哦，我们对所有内容都拥有版权，好吧，我们确实没有，因为我们不拥有作品的版权，它已经在公共领域。但是，我们要做的是根据知识共享零许可证发行整个电子书，这基本上是公共领域。这本书本身包括我们的生产者编写的元数据，书的描述等内容。他们整理了诸如柔纸和印记之类的内容，这些内容是添加的内容，而不是原始公共领域文本的一部分。因此，我们的生产者生产的所有东西在理论上都是他们或组织的版权，因此我们将所有这些东西都发布到公共领域，以便我们可以说整个电子书文件从头到尾都包括在其中。至少在美国的公共领域，如果不是在全球范围内。
  大卫：很明显，自您五六年前开始以来，这个项目就在不断发展。您对我们未来的标准电子书有何挑战？
 亚历克斯：我认为最大的挑战将是寻找资金。就像我之前提到的那样，我们刚刚开始接受税收抵扣的捐赠，那是在12月。真的，就在几个月前。我们要说的是2021年2月。
 获得捐款很重要，因为这正在成为一个越来越耗时的项目。我花大量时间进行管理工作，以协调所有不同阶段的书籍中的所有志愿者，即使我从像您这样的经验丰富的制片人以及其他一些人那里得到了很多帮助，在协调项目上花费了大量时间。有人可能会在邮件列表中问一个问题，例如：“该行带有这种标点符号，我不确定该怎么做。”而这个简单的问题可能是一个单行的答案，但得到答案可能会花一个小时，因为您必须仔细阅读这本书。您必须看到，这是一种特殊情况，有时您必须进行一些研究，因此即使我用一个或两个单词来回信，也可能意味着写一封电子邮件要花一个小时，所以它只会在那天，我仍然有一份全职工作。我的意思是，我经营一家公司，所以这并不是说我有100％的空闲时间。
 因此，这是组织面临的最大挑战，如何以一种可以使我成为全职工作的方式获得资金，并开始雇用其他人来承担一些工作量，而不仅仅是志愿者，因为志愿者非常好，但是我认为一个真正健康的组织可以幸免于难，他们可以全力以赴。
  大卫：是的，而且必须有一些固定费用，例如托管和带宽。所有这些事情。 
亚历克斯：完全正确，所以我的意思是，这些年来我一直在自负支付托管费用，虽然这笔钱不算多，但我的意思不是什么。
 您知道，我也想花时间开发电子书制作流程的基础架构。目前，我们在Google网上论坛的邮件列表中，这让我非常紧张，因为Google可以随时在其任何产品上拔掉插头。他们可能会说我们要关闭网上论坛，然后一个月后您就被困了。因此其基础设施。诸如使我们自己的邮件列表软件运行，使某种项目管理解决方案运行等事情使我们比在邮件列表中仅一个线程有更好的方法来管理这些项目。设立公共领域的艺术资料交换所。
 对于我们所有的电子书生产商来说，最复杂，最耗时的任务之一就是寻找艺术品。因为您必须在这个复杂的过程中证明它在公共领域。因此，拥有一个票据交换所，您可以说这里的所有艺术品都已经过我们的验证，可以接受，这对于许多人来说可以节省大量时间。但是将其放在一起也是一个巨大的项目。这不是您一个月内可以做的事情。
   亚历克斯：所有这些事情都需要金钱或时间，而我现在都没有，所以一旦我们开始赚钱，便可以着手解决所有这些问题，以改善组织，改善生产流程，帮助我们的生产者，诸如此类的事情。
   在我们开始之前，我只是想也许您可能想谈谈其他项目，例如Scribophile。
 Alex：是的，Scribophile是我的主要业务。我已经运行13、14年了。有点久了。从根本上说，这是一个在线写作社区，作家在此注册，然后发表文章进行写作，其他作家则为他们提供有关如何改进它的建议。因此，这是一个批评性的跑步社区。而且，我们是最大的在线独立写作社区之一。我们有很多成员，而且一直以来-我们的成员一直在写有价值的评论小说。每天基本上都会写数十万个批评词，互相帮助。这就是我一直在努力的。这是我的日常工作。
 需要花费时间来维护和管理它。这是一个庞大的社区，因此很多人每天都需要解决许多各种各样的问题。因此，当我不在编写标准电子书时，会花很多时间在上面。 
而且Writerfolio是一家规模较小的企业，可以在线提供服务-我将其称为自由作家的投资组合网站。 因此，如果您是自由撰稿人，并且想要炫耀您的作品，那么与其获得一个可能非常复杂的WordPress网站，不如进行它的构建，它可能需要大量的自定义操作，并且要花费大量时间才能使您的网站正确无误 ，您可以进入Writerfolio，我们提供了各种模板，您可以快速填写这些模板，这些模板专门用于帮助作家展示他们的作品。 因此，这两件事使我头顶蒙上了一层阴影。  大卫：好的。 好吧，Alex。 这真的很有趣，标准电子书是一个很棒的项目。 自从我发现它以来，我当然很享受其中的一部分  ......

David: So I’m talking today with Alex Cabal, who’s the founder and Editor-in-Chief of Standard Ebooks, which is a voluntary organization dedicated to producing beautiful, well formatted ebooks of classic works of literature, and that’s the whole philosophy behind the organization.
 Just a bit of a background about yourself first, Alex. Where did you grow up? How did you come to where you are today?
 Alex: Well, I grew up in the San Francisco Bay Area, and spent my youth there and then I went to university in California in Los Angeles. And I studied computer science and English. I studied. I did both, with a minor in English. After university, I went into a job as a computer programmer, a web developer, and I didn’t quite like this sort of nine to five situation, so I decided to start my own business, which is  Scribophile. That’s my main source of income.
 And after that I took the opportunity to work remotely. This is probably 13 or 14 years ago, so it was not nearly as common as it is today, and so I was one of the very early – I guess you could call them digital nomads that now you hear about all the time. When I was doing that 13, 14 years ago, I was one of the few people doing it at the time, and so I spent a lot of time travelling through Europe, through Australia, New Zealand, Asia, other places. South America. And once I felt like I was done with that, I came to Chicago, where a lot of my family lives. And I decided to settle down there. So I’ve been here for a while now in Chicago.
  David: Okay. So what gave you the idea of starting  Standard Ebooks? We’ll talk a little bit later about Scribophile. But what was the impetus to start Standard Ebooks, which I think you’ve described as a hobby?
 Alex: Yeah, it’s basically a hobby right now. I mean, we only recently started accepting donations, but it’s not like there’s a huge amount of money coming in, so it’s definitely still in the hobby level. And I started it because when I was travelling I was living in Germany for a while – a year or two – and it was very difficult to find English language books there. You have to be in a big city and I wasn’t in a big city.
 And so I had my e-reader at the time. I think it was a Nook at the time. And I started buying books from the Amazon Store, from the Nook Store and the formatting was just really bad on all of them. Again, this is probably more than eight years ago, probably ten or so years ago, and at the time, a lot of the back catalogue books that they had for sale, even for the major publishers, were just bad OCR – optical character recognition – transcriptions, and so they would be filled with typos and errors, and so I’d be sitting there really mad that I’m really paying a lot of money for this book that is not really very good quality at all. You would never print this on paper. It would be embarrassing.
 So I started collecting them for my own library, not to distribute, but just for myself, because I bought these books and may as well keep a nice edition for myself and then over time I decided to download some stuff from  Project Gutenberg, because everyone knows that it’s a great source for a huge variety of classic literature and other public domain literature, and so, I started downloading those ebooks and transferring them into my e-reader. And I realized that although they weren’t at the level of raw OCR – they were fairly good quality in terms of typos and stuff like that, but they were clearly not formatted for what an e-reader can do. And so you’re reading these books that – the typography of them was more akin to a webpage than it was to a print book, even though you’re reading them on an E-Ink device that’s  supposed to be like a print book. So I took the jumping off point that I had with doing my best to edit these commercial ebooks for my own library. And I said to myself, why don’t I just do this for these public domain books and the benefit of doing that is that I can just give them away again? I can set up a website and distribute them and then everyone can enjoy these well-produced books that I was doing for myself, but really, anyone would be interested in getting a copy that is produced to this kind of standard.
 And so that’s kind of how I got started and I had been doing them for myself for maybe a year, just kind of on and off, before I decided to sit down and set up a website and start distributing them officially. So I set up the website, probably I want to say six years ago, five years ago or something like that.
 And it got featured on   Hacker News, which brought in a lot of traffic. It got all over Reddit and a bunch of blogs and news outlets. And everyone seemed to really like the project and from then on I just kind of decided to make a hobby out of it and then here we are many years later and hundreds of ebooks later and hundreds of contributors from all over the world later, it’s become a very big and I’d say successful effort that people enjoy, so I’m very proud of that.
  David: I think you have a great deal to be proud of. You’re coming up to nearly 500 books on the site. Which is interesting in a sense. It’s a mixture of books of different origins, different genres. They are all public domain books. But how did  you decide which books you were going to start to work on and what was the choice of the first few books you decided to work on as official Standard Ebooks productions?
 Alex: Well, the original books are basically ones that I wanted to read for myself. So it was all kind of within my own personal taste of things that are interesting. I don’t remember which one is the very first one, but the very early ones were   Dracula, and   The Enchiridion by Epictetus, which nowadays we would probably put in a compilation because it’s very short, but because it was one of the very first books that we made, it’s just on its own now.
    Some other ones were   Princess of Mars. That was one of the early ones. Books like that, and so they’re kind of all over the place. Kind of a span of philosophy and classic fiction, and then also whatever modern fiction – I personally enjoy science fiction a lot – we can fit in that is still public domain.
 I wish people wouldn’t call them classics because that’s not always the case. I mean we definitely do have a lot of classics that are the kinds of books that you might read in school or that everyone has read at some point and so they have some kind of cultural influence. The books we do are all public domain, but they can be a lot newer, so we have a lot of sci-fi books on there from the 50s and even 60s that fell into the US public domain for various reasons.
    There’s some other modern stuff on there, and also a lot of more scarce stuff that I wouldn’t call classics at all. Just because they’re old doesn’t mean they are classics. I mean, there are philosophy books, or other fiction books that became obscure for whatever reason, but are still very interesting. So we have all kinds of books on there, and our Wanted List originally was books  I wanted to read, so my hope with that Wanted List was that other people would do the hard work for me, and then I could sit down to read the books. But as I’ve been working on this project, people are pulling those books off the list faster than I can read them, and then I’m also reading my own books that I produce for the project. So I’ve realized that I’m never going to have time to read any of these books anyway, so –
  David: Well, it’s getting the stage where it would be pretty tough to read the entire catalogue, that’s for sure.
 Alex: Right, so it’s pretty much out of the question I think at this point.
  David: So I guess the books that are chosen to work on – it’s really up to the individual producer isn’t it? – it’s their individual tastes that get reflected in the catalogue.
  So Standard Ebooks has a collections policy of a sort, which is we basically accept all kinds of fiction. We try to maintain compilations of shorter work where possible, so with someone like H. P. Lovecraft, who wrote a lot of short horror stories, instead of having each one as an individual ebook, we just compile them into one omnibus of all this short fiction. So you don’t have to go scrabbling around in downloading hundreds of files. And then we accept some that I call timeless non-fiction, which is things like philosophy and some autobiography, a limited set of biography, that kind of thing. I think that’s about it. And then we accept plays, that sort of thing. It kind of falls under the fiction genre.
 But as you said, it’s basically whatever the producer personally wants to produce. That’s what they work on because they have to read it, and so they have to have an interest in reading it, and a lot of times that means committing to a really long book. And if they’re not interested, then why bother?
  David: Yes, that’s right. Well, having just completed one of the longest works in English literature,   Clarissa Harlowe, I would never have read it if it hadn’t been for this project.
 Alex: That’s right, you did. I looked at the word count on the website and, it’s almost twice as long as   War and Peace.
     David: Yeah, it’s a pretty long book and yet it is a single work.
   David: I’m glad to have finished it, but I’m also glad to have read it. It was a very good book. Anyway, I don’t think I would have read it if it weren’t for doing it for the project.
 But the other thing I guess is that producers like myself do discover books this way. I don’t think I’d read any  George Eliot before I came to Standard Ebooks. I was looking through one of those  Guardian lists of the “Best 100 Novels in English” and I saw   Middlemarch by George Eliot. I thought ‘that sounds like it’s, people think it’s pretty good’, and so I did that. And that’s how I came to discover George Eliot. And I’m really glad I did. She’s a terrific writer. That’s good.
 Alex: Actually, I haven’t had much of a chance to read George Eliot, so  Middlemarch is definitely something that was probably on the Wanted List at some point, but now is still on my personal To Read list. Eventually, once I get around to it, yeah?
  David: Sure, yeah. Well I’d go for that rather than  The Mill on the Floss, which is a bit little bit dull I think, but  Middlemarch is pretty good.
 Now I wanted to ask you about the look of the books and there’s a very distinct look because you’ve emphasised the idea of making really good looking covers and there’s a certain approach that you’ve taken. Do you want to talk about that? The look of the books and how you came to decide on that and how you maintain that policy.
 Alex: Sure. All of our covers are drawn from art that is also in the public domain. Usually like fine art, oil-painting style art. As I was doing these ebooks for myself, I was thinking that obviously the cover is a big part of a book. And a lot of these books that you download from Project Gutenberg – which was where I was originally downloading them from – have these auto-generated covers that in my opinion, are not very attractive. They were just kind of these random geometric shapes and very bright garish colors and then the author and book title are on there with not a whole lot of thought to positioning or layout.
 Anyway, I thought to myself, maybe we can do something nicer, and if we’re already working on public domain books, why not try to find public domain art that would kind of match that theme of the book, and that’s kind of the general style of the covers. A producer is required to find a public domain fine art painting that in some way reflects the contents of the book and that just becomes the cover. And then we have a very simple black square with the title and author under that and it’s in the same position for all the books. And so just like the name of the project, it becomes kind of the standard where you can you see this cover like a fine art painting, and then that black square with the particular fonts in the name of the book and author. And then you can say, oh, it’s probably a Standard Ebook.
 I mean there’s not a big science to it. I just kind of sat down one day and said these look kind of nice. I’ve been a web designer for a long time and I wouldn’t say that I’m an especially gifted arts kind of person. I am not so much into the graphic design arena, but I spent a lot of time working on these websites so I think part of that came from there, where you just kind of have an eye on margins and padding and what kind of looks attractive and what doesn’t. And people seem to like the covers so –
  David: Yeah, absolutely. There’s certainly a very distinctive and a very attractive look to them.
 Each of the books that you produced, you release or dedicate to the public domain, that was obviously a philosophy right from the start. Is that because the material starts in the public domain? Other organisations or other people might think, ‘Oh, I can make money out of doing this’, but you obviously decided that wasn’t the approach.
 Alex: Yes, well, I don’t think there’s a lot of money in public domain books, honestly. If you go on Amazon, any search for  Dracula there’s like 5,000 crappy editions that someone has put together for an ebook, and they’re all free or like $0.99.
 So there’s not a huge money in public domain ebooks. I would say public domain print books, there might be some marketplaces like Dover for example, who make money off that.
   Alex: You can make educational editions or annotated editions like Penguin Classics or something like that, and so that’s a different story, but as far as ebooks goes, I never thought there is going to be any money in it, and there hasn’t been.
 As far as the public domain dedication goes, the contents of the book themselves are already in the US public domain, so it’s not like our dedication changes that in any way. Even if we had sat down and said, oh, we’ve copyrighted everything, well, we really haven’t because we don’t own the rights to the work to begin with, it’s already in the public domain. But what we do is we release the entire ebook under the  Creative Commons Zero license, which is basically public domain. The book itself includes things like metadata that our producers write, descriptions of the books; they arrange things like colophons and imprints which are additions and are not part of the original public domain text. So all that stuff that our producers produce is in theory copyright by them or by the organization, and so we release all of that to the public domain so that we can say that this entire ebook file from start to finish, everything included, is at least in the US public domain, if not the worldwide public domain.
  David: So obviously this project has grown and grown since you began it five or six years ago. What do you see as the challenges for Standard Ebooks as we go into the future?
 Alex: I think the big challenge is going to be finding funding. Like I mentioned earlier that we just started to accept donations on a tax deductible basis, and that was in December. So really, just a few months ago. We’re talking February right now of 2021.
 Getting donations is important because this is becoming an increasingly time consuming project. I’m spending a lot of time doing management work for coordinating all of these volunteers through all these various stages of books, and even though I have a lot of help from our experienced producers like yourself, and a few other people, there’s still a lot of time spent in just coordinating the project. Someone might ask a question in the mailing list saying something like, ‘This line has this kind of punctuation and I’m not sure what to do about it.’ And that simple question might be a one line answer but getting that answer out could take an hour because you have to go and look through the book. You have to see, is this kind of a special case, you have to do some research sometimes and so even if I reply back with like one or two words, it could signal an hour to write that email, so it just adds up during the day and I have a full time job anyway. I mean, I run a business and so it’s not like I have 100% free time.
 And so that’s the big challenge of the organization, being able to be funded in a way where I could make this a full-time job and start hiring other people to take on some of this workload on not just a volunteer basis because, well, volunteers are very good, but I think a really healthy organization survives off people who can dedicate their entire time to it.
  David: Yeah, and there must be some fixed costs like hosting and bandwidth. All those sorts of things.
 Alex: Exactly right, so I mean, I’ve been paying hosting costs out of pocket all these years, and it’s not a huge amount, but I mean it’s not nothing.
 You know, I would also like to spend time on developing the infrastructure of the ebook production process. Right now we’re on a Google Groups mailing list, and that makes me really nervous because Google can pull the plug at any time on any of their products. They could say we’re shutting down Groups and then a month later you’re stuck. So its infrastructure. Things like getting our own mailing list software going, getting some kind of project management solution going so we have a better way to manage these projects than just one thread on a mailing list. Setting up a public domain art clearing house.
 One of the most complicated and time consuming tasks, for all of our ebook producers, is finding art. Because you have to prove it’s in the public domain in this complicated process. So having a single clearing house where you could say all the art here has been verified by us to be acceptable for use would be a huge time saver for a lot of people. But putting it together is also a huge project. It’s not something you can do in a month so.
   Alex: All of these things require money or time, and I don’t have either of those right now, so once we once we start getting money in, then we can start addressing all these things to improve the organization, improve the production process, help our producers, that sort of thing.
   Before we go I just thought perhaps you might like to talk about your other projects like Scribophile.
 Alex: Yeah, Scribophile is my sort of main business. I’ve been running it for 13, 14 years now. Kind of a long time. And it’s basically an online writing community where writers sign up and then they post to work in progress, and other writers give them advice on how to improve it. So it’s a critique running community. And we’re one of the largest independent writing communities online. We have a whole lot of members and we’ve been – our members have been writing novels-worth of critiques. Every day basically hundreds of thousands of words of critique is written, helping each other improve. And that’s what I’ve been working on. It’s my day job.
 It takes up time to maintain and to manage it. It’s a large community, so a lot of people have a lot of various problems that they need to get sorted out over the course of the day. And so I spend a lot of my time on that when I’m not working on Standard Ebooks.
 And  Writerfolio is a smaller business that provides online – I would call it portfolio websites for freelance writers. So if you’re a freelance writer and you want to show off your work, then instead of getting a WordPress site which could be very complicated, it could require a lot of customization, a lot of time consumed trying to get your website just right, you can come to Writerfolio, and we have various templates that you can fill out very quickly that are specifically geared to helping writers show off their work. So those are the two things that sort of keep a roof over my head.
  David: All right. Well thanks Alex. It’s been really interesting and Standard Ebooks is a great project. I’ve certainly personally have enjoyed enormously being part of it since I discovered it a few ye
......

Dio Cassius的描述是讲述：在Regio V挖掘的空间很可能实际上是一条酒馆。尽管许多出版物是指Pompeii的餐饮场所作为热电基利，但LOEB古典图书馆只记录了正在使用的术语的两个实例。专家，如吨Kleberg，Mary Beard，Steven Ellis和Claire Holleran都认为，拉丁文学记录Popina的更常见的术语是这些类型的空间的更合适的名称。通常被翻译为“小酒馆”，它有时被翻译为“公共房屋”，或者在更现代化的白话中，酒吧。要想象古罗马酒馆的气氛，人们可以简单地看看仍然装饰庞贝城的空间墙壁的壁画，描绘了饮酒，树花，赌博和马戏的场景。
  考虑到一些证据，如果我们要假设我们在拉丁文学记录中读到的“煮沸的肉”，“肉汤和肉汤”和炖圈的雷克和粪便“是指的Popina，那么我们还可以推测在Regio V中新出土Popina的Dolium中发现的骨骼和壳质注定为肉汤。所有这些证据表明，在角落波利纳肉汤中煮沸的肉是罗马版的酒吧grub？我相信它是这样做的，这现在让我们更接近提出一堂罗马餐，这可能让我们使用另一个调查工具来探索这个空间：我们的味蕾。
     在他的烹饪书籍De Coquinaria中，一世纪的Gourmand Marcus Gavius Apicius以下列方式准备鸭子或起重机。角落Popina可能看起来有点高昂，但它很简单，是APICian标准，并具有一些常用的成分和时代的风味增强剂。
 洗衣服（鸟）并放入一个大型烹饪锅;加水，盐，莳萝;煮它直到它坚定，中途穿过烹饪过程;把它拿出来放入另一个锅中，用油和酸甜，用一束牛至和香菜。当几乎烹饪时，添加了一些Defrutum以添加颜色。牛胡椒，可爱，小茴香，香菜，激光根，rue，caroenum，蜂蜜，倒在一些烹饪酒，醋味。将此倒回到平底锅中，使其变暖。用淀粉加厚。把鸟放在一盘上，然后倒酱。
 为了陪前，我选择包括Mensae，曾经认为用作板块和器具的平坦面包。 Virgil描述了在Aeneid中这种方式享受mensae：埃涅阿斯和他的男人在扁平面包上吃饭后，他们的评论，“哦，看！我们也在吃我们的表！“
   我决定使用我的兵马俑（便携式火车）和ollae（烹饪罐）来为这个配方来模拟原来的烹饪技术和西塞罗的炖屋的“雷克和烟雾”，尽我所能。但我的便携式厨房实际上可能更适合Juvenal讽刺的奴隶之一，而不是Popina的固定壁炉的替代品。家庭厨师可以在烧烤煤或木材上使用粘土锅和三脚架，或在常规炉灶上的常规炊具，以实现类似的结果。
 我烘烤了我的一些扁平面包Sub Testu-ineard-iscamic testum或clibanus  - 其中一些是在植物上制备的，该技术由Deipnosophistae中的Athenaeus引用。理解Popina内的工作和感官环境的一部分涉及使用相同的烹饪技术和相同的燃料。然而，使用现代技术的家庭烹饪将在炉灶或烤架上遇到这个食谱，但是，你不会因为我所做的那样离开它的抹菜和鸭子脂肪。 
对于红烧鸭：2个鸭胸或鸭腿一小束莳萝或1茶匙（2克）干莳萝少许盐2汤匙（20克）橄榄油2茶匙（6克）Colatura d'alici或红船鱼露一小束新鲜的牛至或1茶匙（2克）干牛至一小束新鲜的香菜或1茶匙（2克）干香菜3汤匙（60克）果皮/胡萝卜使用此食谱）或购买的糖蜜1汤匙（20克）红酒醋1茶匙（5克）蜂蜜1茶匙（5克）切成丁的蒲公英绿（意大利的cicoria）来代替粗（可能有毒）大容量）½茶匙（2克）干黑胡椒粉；独活小茴香香菜; asafoetida（也称为铰链，在大多数亚洲和印度市场或保健食品商店都可以买到）代替激光（sil）1汤匙（15克）面粉1汤匙（15克）鸭脂，猪油或无盐黄油新鲜牛至（用于装饰）
 对于月经：2杯（250克）石磨全麦粉1/4杯（60克）酵母面包发酵剂¾杯（160克）水½茶匙（2克）盐橄榄油（如果煎炸和不烤）
 手上没有入门者？用1茶匙（5克）商业面包酵母制作一个由面粉和水等份组成的小海绵。在配方中使用1/4杯或60克这种海绵。
   准备用于月经的面团：将发酵剂溶解在水中，与面粉和盐混合，然后揉成面团，盖上面团，在温暖的地方静置一小时。
 将鸭子放入锅中，浸入水中，加入莳萝和少许盐，然后煮沸。盖上锅中火煮45分钟，制成清汤。如果您觉得自己很勇敢，可在汤中加入一些蜗牛壳，山羊骨头和猪骨头，以增添风味。
 待月经面面团休息后，将面团切成两半，然后将每半折叠成一个球。用a面杖或手掌将每个球压成圆盘。用湿的茶巾盖上盖子，让面团再静置30分钟。
 在锅中，将橄榄油和鱼露与牛至和香菜混合，然后中火加热。 
将鸭子从高汤锅中取出，与油，鱼露和草药一起在锅中烤熟。淋上一半的果皮（或葡萄糖蜜）。鸭子变成棕色后，将其从锅中取出并放在一边。将滴水保持在锅中。
 在碗中，将剩余的果渣（或葡萄糖蜜）与红酒醋，蜂蜜，切成丁的蒲公英青菜（或二氧化铈），黑胡椒粉，独活草，小茴香，香菜和细辛混合在一起，一起搅拌。
 在锅中，将面粉和鸭脂肪（或猪油或无盐黄油）加到滴水中，并通过在低火下将面粉和脂肪溶解在一起而制成面包。用打蛋器防止结块。
 将蜂蜜，醋和香料的混合物与1杯（215克）鸭肉汤混合，然后在低火下将液体慢慢加到锅中的肉汤中，搅拌均匀，直到开始变稠成为酱汁为止。
 通过用中高火加热烧烤架或煎锅来煮两个月经。将每个面糊放在热的烤架上或平移并烤，直到开始膨胀为止。然后将其翻转并烤另一面直到金黄色。如果在充气前月经太快褐变，请降低热量。
 在每道菜上放一大勺酱汁。将鸭肉切成一口大小的肉，放在酱汁上。淋上细雨，再用小汤包住鸭肉，并点缀新鲜牛至的小树枝。
 将烤好的月经切成薄片，放在切好的鸭肉旁边，浸泡酱汁和肉汤。 
现在拿碗红烧鸭和面包，想像一下您是庞贝古城的凉亭。在光线充足的地方找到凳子，看看您面前的食物。您可能需要在一个陌生人旁边闲逛，以确保您的零钱包安全。最好把它当作最后一杯酒。当您运动过多时，地面总是在您的脚下颤抖，并且现在正在这样做。不用担心，肉汤和面包会把你吓得发麻，刚好错开那只狗的脚，那只狗不会停在远处的东西上。挠头分散他的注意力，然后说再见。在仍有阳光的时候走了。是白天吗？外面的空气有一种奇怪的黄色调和刺鼻的气味，当您将自己稳定在喷泉的边缘时，大地似乎仍在周期性地发抖。可能只是酒，但您最好快点回家。维苏威火山上空形成了乌云，看起来好像有暴风雨来临。
 *更正：本文最初指出，普林尼在公元79年写了这封信。火山喷发后数十年，即公元106年左右。

In the second century, Pliny the Younger wrote a letter to the Roman historian Tacitus, recounting the early stages of the eruption of Mount Vesuvius in 79 AD.* From his villa in Misenum, across the Bay of Naples from the volcano, he remembered seeing a dark cloud, shaped like an umbrella pine tree, filling the sky over the mountains flanking the northern edge of Sarno River plain. What followed was something that no one in the region was prepared for. A day after Pliny observed that dark cloud, a small tavern in a northeastern section of Pompeii collapsed, along with the rest of the town, under the weight of pumice and ash. This was later followed by a fast-moving pyroclastic surge of hot gas, volcanic debris, and ash that signaled the volcano’s final devastating blow: Those who stayed behind in Pompeii and Herculaneum were killed instantly by this infernal wave of heat, estimated to have been as high as 900° Fahrenheit. The barkeep of this tavern was one of these poor souls. He didn’t make it out of the establishment in time and perished in the cot where he slept, along with a dog and a man who had taken refuge inside the tavern with them.
  In December 2020, archaeologists at the Archaeological Park of Pompeii announced that they had found the remains of these two men and the dog as they were excavating this ancient food establishment, known as a  thermopolium. Located in Regio V, on the western edge of the unexcavated northeastern section of Pompeii , the well-preserved thermopolium’s counter, ceramic storage containers (known as  dolia), and wall art offer some of the most pristine and moving finds to be discovered in recent years at the site.
 To begin, the space’s large masonry counter was adorned with frescoes depicting scenes of daily life inside the venue, such as the flagons and cooking implements hung above the bar and the image of a porter making a delivery, as well as a portrait of a Greek sea nymph riding a seahorse and images of mallards, a chicken, and a dog. Leaning against the bar were several ceramic wine jars, known as  amphorae, which originally housed locally produced and imported Greek wines. Lastly, some of the dolia contained the bones of several types of animals and one  dolium contained remains belonging to one of the men (in the case of the latter, it’s likely they were placed there by 18th-century looters).
  As a classical archaeologist whose research centers on food and food preparation in the Roman Mediterranean, I am overjoyed by finds like these, as the information obtained from them shines a bright light on the daily lives of classes of Roman society that are poorly represented in ancient literary sources: slaves and average, working Romans. Spaces like this thermopolium provide archaeologists like me with a realistic portrayal of what Roman food culture was like in comparison to sensational portrayals of Roman food culture, such as those found in satirical literary sources like Petronius’s “Trimalchio’s Banquet” or portrayed in opulent frescoes like those adorning the dining-room walls of the House of the Vettii, an exceptionally well-preserved luxury  domus.
 In contrast, this thermopolium invites us into an archaeological environment that gives an indication of where many everyday Pompeiians enjoyed cooked meals. According to Dr. Anna Maria Sodo, director and archaeology officer of the Antiquarium of Boscoreale, in the Vesuvian area alone, only 40 percent of the urban dwellings of the working poor and 66 percent of the middle-class homes had fixed hearths for cooking. To meet this high demand, there were  at least 80 food and beverage outlets at Pompeii (the site has yet to be fully excavated). But what types of foods did these thermopolia serve to the everyday citizens?
  To recreate a meal that comes close to what may have been served here, one can begin by looking at the archaeological remains found inside. This includes the imagery in the frescoes painted on the service counter and the contents of the amphorae and the dolia. One dolium contained the bones of duck, swine, goat, and fish, as well as shells from land snails. The duck bones in particular correspond to the fresco of two mallards painted on the front of the counter, perhaps as a pictorial menu for the illiterate majority at the time. While some scholars have proposed that these faunal remains may imply that first-century Pompeiians consumed stews or soups  composed of a motley of creatures, aside from a few Apician  patinae, this would be an anomaly in ancient Roman cooking. As dolia were primarily used for dry and liquid food storage, and not for cooking, it is possible that the bones and shells found in the dolium represented food waste from an in-house butchery, food preparation that took place behind the counter, or post-consumption food waste left behind by the customers.
 It is also possible that these remains were evidence of something else entirely: What if these shells and bones were destined for a master stock that was used in the dishes being served in the establishment? After all, this thermopolium was situated near a fountain and water tower, providing access that would facilitate making a bulk broth on a daily basis. References to such dishes appear across historic texts: In the first century BC, Republican statesman Cicero complains of the “reek and fume” of stew-houses in his biting invective  Against Piso, while Athenaeus of Naucratis, a third-century Greco-Egyptian writer, refers to food in the “common messes” as “nothing but broth and chunks of meat.” Second-century historian Dio Cassius even recounts an occasion when Emperor Claudius “abolished the taverns where [the populace] were wont to gather and drink, and commanded that no boiled meat or hot water should be sold.”
  Dio Cassius’s description is telling: It’s highly likely that the space excavated in Regio V was, in fact, a tavern. Despite the fact that many publications refer to the dining establishments at Pompeii as thermopolia, the Loeb Classical Library records only two instances of the term being used. Experts such as Tonnes Kleberg, Mary Beard, Steven Ellis, and Claire Holleran have all noted that the more frequently found term in the Latin literary record,  popina, is a more suitable name for these types of spaces. Typically translated as “tavern,” it’s sometimes translated as “public-house,” or, in the more modern vernacular, pub. To imagine the atmosphere of ancient Roman taverns, one can simply look to the frescoes that still adorn the walls of such spaces in Pompeii, depicting scenes of drinking, canoodling, gambling, and horseplay.
  In consideration of some of this evidence, if we were to hypothesize that what we’ve read in the Latin literary record about “boiled meat,” “broth and chunks of meat,” and the “reek and fume” of stew houses refers to a popina, then we can also speculate that the bone and shell matter found in the dolium in the newly unearthed popina in Regio V was destined for broth. Does all of this evidence suggest that meat boiled in broth at the corner popina is the Roman version of pub grub? I believe it does, and this now brings us much closer to proposing a first-century Roman meal that could allow us to explore this space using another investigative tool: our taste buds.
     In his cookbook  De Re Coquinaria, the first-century gourmand Marcus Gavius Apicius prepares duck or crane in broth in the following manner. It may seem a bit upmarket for a corner popina, but it is simple by Apician standards and features some of the commonly used ingredients and flavor enhancers of the time.
 Wash and dress (the bird) and put in a large cooking pot; add water, salt, dill; cook it until it is firm, halfway through the cooking process; take it out and put it in another pan with oil and liquamen and with a bundle of oregano and coriander. When it is almost cooked add a little defrutum to add colour. Pound pepper, lovage, cumin, coriander, laser root, rue, caroenum, honey, pour on some of the cooking liquor, flavour with vinegar. Pour this back into the pan so that it warms through. Thicken with starch. Put the bird on a serving dish and pour the sauce over.
 To accompany the main dish, I chose to include  mensae, a flatbread that was once thought to be used as both a plate and a utensil. Virgil describes enjoying mensae this way in   The Aeneid: After Aeneas and his men eat their meals off the flatbread, they remark, “Oh, look! we are eating our tables too!”
   I decided to use my terracotta  foculus (portable brazier) and  ollae (cooking pots) for this recipe in order to simulate the original cooking technologies and the “reek and fume” of Cicero’s stew-houses to the best of my ability. But my portable kitchen may actually be more suited to one of the slaves in Juvenal’s  Satires than as a substitute for a fixed hearth in a popina. Home cooks can use a clay pot and tripod over barbecue coals or wood, or regular cookware on their conventional stoves to achieve a similar result.
 I baked some of my flatbreads  sub testu—beneath a ceramic  testum or  clibanus—and some were prepared on a griddle, a technique referenced by Athenaeus in the  Deipnosophistae. Part of understanding the working and sensory environment inside a popina involves using the same cooking technologies and the same fuels. Home cooks using modern technologies, however, will have little difficulty making this recipe at home on a stovetop or grill, but you won’t come away from it smelling of woodsmoke and duck fat, as I did.
   For the braised duck:  2 duck breasts or duck legs  A small bunch of dill or 1 teaspoon (2 grams) of dried dill  Pinch of salt  2 tablespoons (20 grams) of olive oil  2 teaspoons (6 grams) of colatura d’alici  or Red Boat fish sauce  A small bunch of fresh oregano or 1 teaspoon (2 grams) of dried oregano  A small bunch of fresh coriander or 1 teaspoon (2 grams) of dried coriander  3 tablespoons (60 grams) defrutum/caroenum ( which you can make with this recipe) or store-bought grape molasses  1 tablespoon (20 grams) red wine vinegar  1 teaspoon (5 grams) honey  1 teaspoon (5 grams) diced dandelion greens (cicoria,  in Italian) to substitute for rue (which is potentially toxic in high volumes)  ½ teaspoon (2 grams) each of dried ground black pepper; lovage; cumin; coriander; asafoetida (also known as hing and available at most Asian and Indian markets or health-food stores) to substitute for laser (silphium)  1 tablespoon (15 grams) flour  1 tablespoon (15 grams) duck fat, lard, or unsalted butter  Sprigs of fresh oregano (for garnish)
 For the mensae:  2 cups (250 grams) of stone-ground whole wheat flour  1/4 cup (60 grams) of sourdough bread starter  ¾ cup (160 grams) of water  ½ teaspoon (2 grams) salt  Olive oil (if frying and not grilling)
 No starter on hand? Make a small sponge composed of equal parts flour and water with 1 teaspoon (5 grams) of commercial baker’s yeast. Use ¼ cup or 60 grams of this sponge for the recipe.
   Prepare the dough for the mensae : Dissolve the starter in the water, combine with the flour and salt, then knead the dough, cover it, and let it rest for one hour in a warm place.
 Place the duck in a pot, submerge it in water, add the dill and pinch of salt, and bring it to a boil. Cover and simmer on medium-low for 45 minutes to create a light broth. If you’re feeling brave, add a few snail shells, goat bones, and pork bones to the broth for added flavor.
 After the mensae dough has rested, cut the dough in half, and fold each half into a ball. Using a rolling pin or the palm of your hand, flatten each ball into a disk. Cover with a damp tea towel and let the dough rest for another 30 minutes.
 In a pan, combine the olive oil and fish sauce with the oregano and coriander, then heat on medium-high.
 Remove the duck from the broth pot and sear it in the pan along with the oil, fish sauce, and herbs. Drizzle with half of the defrutum (or grape molasses). Once the duck has browned, remove it from the pan and set it aside. Keep the drippings in the pan.
 In a bowl, combine the remaining defrutum (or grape molasses) with the red wine vinegar, honey, diced dandelion greens (or  cicoria), ground black pepper, lovage, cumin, coriander, and asafoetida and whisk it all together.
 In the pan, add the flour and duck fat (or lard or unsalted butter) to the drippings and make a roux by dissolving the flour and fat together on low heat. Use a whisk to prevent clumping.
 Combine the mixture of honey, vinegar, and spices with 1 cup (215 grams) of the duck broth and slowly add the liquid to the roux in the pan, on low heat, whisking it together until it begins to thicken into a sauce.
 Cook both mensae by either heating a grill or a frying pan with olive oil, on medium-high. Place each mensa onto the hot grill or pan and grill it until it starts to inflate. Then flip it over and grill the other side until golden brown. Lower your heat if the mensae are browning too quickly before inflating.
 Place a large dollop of the sauce on each serving dish. Slice the duck meat into bite-size morsels and place them on top of the sauce. Drizzle with additional broth to surround the duck morsels and garnish with sprigs of fresh oregano.
 Slice the grilled mensae into wedges and serve them alongside the sliced duck to soak up the sauce and the broth.
 Now take your bowl of braised duck and your bread, and imagine you’re in a Pompeiian popina. Find a stool where there’s enough light to see the food in front of you. You may have to sidle up next to a stranger so make sure your coin purse is secure. Best make this your last cup of wine. The ground always trembles beneath your feet when you’ve had too much and it’s doing so right now. Not to worry, the broth and bread will sober you up just enough to stagger out the front door past that dog that won’t stop barking at something off in the distance. Scratch his head to distract him, then say goodbye. Time to go while there’s still daylight. Is it daylight? The air outside has a strange yellow hue to it, and an acrid smell, and the earth feels as if it’s still trembling periodically as you steady yourself on the edge of a fountain. It’s probably just the wine but you’d better get home quickly. A dark cloud is forming above  Vesuvio and it looks as if a storm is on its way.
 * Correction: This article originally stated that Pliny wrote the letter in 79 AD. It was several decades after the eruption, around 106 AD.
 Gastro Obscura covers the world’s most wondrous food and drink.  Sign up for our email, delivered twice a week.
